SUMMARY:
For cities, digital twins simulate everything from infrastructure like roads, bridges, and buildings to entire resource management systems like energy grids and water distribution. Digital twins offer cities three key functionalities: real-time data integration, predictive modeling, and resource management. In Singapore, the government has created a comprehensive digital twin of the entire city, which it uses to manage urban development and improve public services. A digital twin is a real-time virtual model of a city's physical infrastructure, used to monitor, simulate, and manage urban systems such as transportation, utilities, and public services. Digital twins help local governments make informed, data-driven decisions, improve resource management, and enhance citizen engagement, leading to more efficient and sustainable city operations.

# The Rise of Digital Twins: How Cities Are Creating Virtual Models for Real-World Impact

![](https://www.govpilot.com/hubfs/The%20Rise%20of%20Digital%20Twins_%20How%20Cities%20Are%20Creating%20Virtual%20Models%20for%20Real-World%20Impact%20%281%29.png)

City [officials constantly grapple with challenges](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/city-government-software-workflows) like managing infrastructure, optimizing resource allocation, and maintaining citizen engagement—all while facing the constraints of limited budgets and evolving urban demands.

The complexities of modern urban management require [data-driven solutions](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/local-government-analytics-data-strategy) that go beyond traditional methods. Enter digital twins, a groundbreaking technology that provides a virtual replica of real-world systems, [enabling cities to model](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/local-government-growth-management), simulate, and optimize urban operations.

For municipalities seeking to leverage these tools, companies like GovPilot are leading the way in integrating cutting-edge technology for efficient city management. **Digital twins offer an unparalleled opportunity to revolutionize how cities plan, respond, and grow**.

This article delves into how digital twins are transforming urban planning and management, with real-world examples of their success. It also explains how [local government officials](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/10-leadership-tips-for-local-government-officials-from-govpilot) can use this technology to make data-driven decisions and why embracing platforms like [GovPilot can streamline the adoption process](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/adopting-modern-technology-into-the-municipal-workplace-govpilot).

## What Are Digital Twins?

A digital twin is a virtual model that mirrors a physical asset, system, or process in real-time. For cities, **digital twins simulate everything from infrastructure like roads, bridges, and buildings** to entire resource management systems like energy grids and water distribution.

These virtual models of cities like your continuously update with [data from Internet of Things](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/what-the-internet-of-things-can-do-govpilot) (IoT) sensors, environmental inputs, and other sources, **allowing city officials to understand, manage, and predict urban dynamics with incredible accuracy**.

[Using platforms like GovPilot](https://www.govpilot.com/), **cities can more easily integrate digital twin technology into their daily operations**. By creating a virtual environment that accurately reflects the current state of urban infrastructure, city managers can simulate potential scenarios, plan for future projects, and address ongoing issues proactively.

Learn all about [Emerging Technologies in Local Government](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/emerging-technologies-in-local-government-what-to-expect-in-2024) in our resource!

## The Basics of Digital Twins in City Management

Digital twins offer cities **three key functionalities**: **real-time data integration, predictive modeling, and resource management**. By using a digital twin, cities can merge real-time data from various sources, such as IoT sensors, and create a unified view of their infrastructure and resources. This capability is vital for monitoring and optimizing systems like transportation, utilities, and public services.

### Real-time Data Integration

**Digital twins continuously receive live data from infrastructure sensors**, traffic systems, and environmental monitors. For example, traffic cameras or smart meters can feed data into the twin, providing city officials with an up-to-the-minute view of urban activity.

Check out our article on [The Future of Civic Tech and Trends in Citizen Engagement](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/the-future-of-civic-tech-trends-in-citizen-engagement-from-govpilot).

### Predictive Modeling

The predictive aspect of digital twins [enables cities to forecast future scenarios](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/future-of-government). **From predicting traffic patterns during peak hours to anticipating the wear and tear of bridges**, digital twins can help prevent problems before they escalate.

Curious about [What is Municipal Software?](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/municipal-software) Learn all of your answers here.

### Resource Management

Efficient use of resources is a crucial benefit of digital twins. [Cities can optimize water distribution](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/modern-local-government-water-utilities-strategy), energy consumption, and waste management, cutting costs and reducing environmental impact.

By integrating with GovPilot, [municipalities can streamline their operations](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/government-automation-101-what-can-your-municipality-automate-govpilot), using predictive insights to reduce waste and optimize their resource management strategies.

## A Deeper Understanding: The Role of Real-Time Data and Predictive Analysis

One of the most **powerful aspects of digital twins is their** [**ability to analyze real-time data**](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/local-government-analytics-data-strategy) **and predict outcomes** that drive more informed decision-making. This section explores how digital twins are [used for traffic management](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/improve-local-public-transportation), infrastructure maintenance, and disaster preparedness, providing local officials with a clear understanding of their benefits.

### Traffic Management

**Real-time data from road sensors and cameras can be fed into digital twins to model traffic patterns and congestion**. This allows city officials to adjust traffic signals, reroute vehicles, and minimize congestion during peak hours. GovPilot has [helped cities implement similar data-driven solutions](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/local-government-data-driven-decision-making) that reduce gridlock and improve overall mobility.

Consider how [Local Transportation Can Improve Upward Mobility](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/learn-how-local-transportation-can-improve-upward-mobility-with-govpilot) here!

### Infrastructure Maintenance

By [monitoring the condition of critical infrastructure](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/new-jersey-infrastructure-bill-grants) like roads, bridges, and pipelines, digital twins provide predictive maintenance alerts. If a bridge is nearing its load-bearing capacity or a water pipe shows signs of deterioration, **city officials can take proactive steps to prevent costly repairs or failures.**

Consider reading our [Modern Public Works Strategy for Local Governments](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/local-government-department-of-public-works-strategy) here!

### Disaster Preparedness

With real-time data from weather stations and environmental sensors, digital twins can simulate natural disasters, such as floods or hurricanes, and predict their impact on infrastructure and residents. This allows cities to implement preemptive strategies, such as evacuations or fortifying vulnerable areas to aid in [disaster preparedness for governments](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/disaster-preparedness-how-local-governments-build-resilient-communities).

Read on about [Disaster Preparedness and  How Local Governments Build Resilient Communities](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/disaster-preparedness-how-local-governments-build-resilient-communities) here!

## How Digital Twins Improve Urban Planning and Infrastructure

[Local urban planning](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/municipal-planning-reclaiming-city-streets) is another area where digital twins shine. Cities can simulate various development projects and infrastructure scenarios to assess long-term impacts before breaking ground.

GOVTIP: Digital twins provide insights into how new infrastructure—like public transportation systems or housing developments—will affect traffic flow, resource consumption, and the surrounding environment.

For example, **a city might use a digital twin to test the placement of a new subway line,** studying its impact on traffic, foot traffic, and local businesses. Similarly, planners can use digital twins to assess the environmental impact of new construction, such as how it might affect water runoff, air quality, or green spaces.

[Municipalities using platforms like GovPilot](https://www.govpilot.com/case-studies) have been able to expedite planning processes while gaining deeper insights into urban sustainability.

In addition to these applications, digital twins also improve infrastructure maintenance and construction efficiency by providing precise simulations and forecasts. This ensures that cities make better-informed decisions, minimizing financial and environmental risks.

## The Benefits of Digital Twins for Citizen Engagement Explained

Digital twins also play a significant role in enhancing citizen engagement.

#### By making digital models accessible to the public city officials can:

For example, cities might **use digital twins to show residents how a new park or community center will fit into the local landscape, encouraging feedback and participation**. Making data available for citizen monitoring allows for greater transparency, as residents can see real-time information on city projects and services.

GovPilot excels in providing solutions that enhance citizen engagement, offering municipalities user-friendly platforms that integrate digital twins with public-facing dashboards.

## Digital Twins Case Studies: Success Stories in City Management

Several cities have already implemented digital twins with great success:

**In Singapore, the government has created a comprehensive digital twin of the entire city**, which it uses to manage urban development and improve public services. The twin helps Singapore monitor traffic, manage energy consumption, and predict the impacts of future development projects.

**New York City has adopted digital twin technology to optimize transportation networks**, manage public infrastructure, and prepare for environmental challenges like rising sea levels. In Helsinki, digital twins are used for public engagement, allowing residents to visualize and provide input on urban development projects before they begin.

These case studies demonstrate the transformative potential of digital twins in urban environments, highlighting the measurable benefits, such as reduced traffic congestion, enhanced public services, and improved disaster preparedness.

## Why Local Governments Should Consider Digital Twins

Digital twins offer numerous advantages for local governments, from **improved decision-making** to [**enhanced citizen engagement**](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/promoting-and-fostering-citizen-engagement-in-government-with-govpilot) and **resource optimization**.

By integrating real-time data, predictive modeling, and citizen input, municipalities can [ensure that their cities are prepared for the future](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/future-of-government).

For local governments looking to adopt digital twins, partnering with experts like GovPilot can streamline the process. [GovPilot's cloud-based systems are designed to support municipalities](https://www.govpilot.com/blog/municipal-software) in leveraging digital twins for greater efficiency, helping them optimize their operations, engage with citizens, and manage resources more effectively. By embracing this technology, cities can stay ahead of the curve and ensure long-term sustainability.

## Digital City FAQs

### What is a digital twin in city management?

A digital twin is a real-time virtual model of a city's physical infrastructure, used to monitor, simulate, and manage urban systems such as transportation, utilities, and public services.

### Why should local governments use digital twins?

Digital twins help local governments make informed, data-driven decisions, improve resource management, and enhance citizen engagement, leading to more efficient and sustainable city operations.

## Artificial Intelligence and Economic-Social Planning: The Power of Digital Twins

Today, [artificial intelligence (AI)](https://www.twinup.io/digital-twin-artificial-intelligence/) technologies are revolutionizing a wide range of fields, from urban planning to economic management. At TwinUp, our goal is to make economic and [social planning](https://www.twinup.io/digital-twin-infrastructure-superstructure/) processes more efficient by creating a [digital twin](https://www.twinup.io/about/) of Konya. Digital twins transfer real-world data into the digital realm, offering more accurate analyses and forecasts about cities. This allows for the development of the right strategies for economic and social growth.

**[Economic Planning](https://www.twinup.io/digital-twin-energy-management/) and Artificial Intelligence**

Artificial intelligence is a powerful tool in economic planning. Digital twins model the existing infrastructure and resources of cities in the digital environment, enabling us to predict future economic trends. Data such as economic growth, labor markets, energy consumption, and more can be analyzed with AI algorithms to optimize urban development strategies.

In this process, municipalities and decision-makers can make faster and more effective decisions through AI-based digital twins. Data-driven analyses help optimize resource use and accelerate economic growth.

**Social Planning and Digital Twins**

Social planning plays a critical role in improving the quality of life in cities. Digital twins allow us to analyze social dynamics within cities, offering the opportunity to plan improvements in education, healthcare, transportation, and other social services more accurately. AI-powered modeling plays an important role in developing solutions tailored to the needs of communities.

Social planning supported by AI not only enhances efficiency but also lays the foundation for a more humane urban life. Making people-centered decisions becomes much easier with the simulations provided by digital twins.

**Conclusion**

At TwinUp, our goal is to contribute to the economic and social development of cities through digital twin technology. With AI-supported analysis, it is possible to design more sustainable, efficient, and human-centered cities. Leveraging the opportunities provided by these technologies that will shape the future will propel our cities forward in every way.

# The value of digital twins

***About this series:** “Digital Twins” is a three-part series that introduces health care facilities managers to the world of digital twins. It offers a clear and practical overview of what digital twins are; the specific value they bring to health care; the role of planning, design and construction (PDC) in their implementation; and how to get started.*

*This first article offers facilities managers a general introduction to digital twins, including a basic definition, their intended audiences and key use cases. Subsequent articles will explore the value of digital twin data in construction handovers and delve deeper into the unique benefits digital twins provide to health care facilities.*

![](https://www.hfmmagazine.com/sites/default/files/2025-02/DigitaTwinProgram_700X468.png)

Digital twins help to bridge the gap between design and construction, and daily operations.

Image courtesy of VIATechnik

Facilities managers are adaptable problem solvers, often referred to as a “rare breed” because, as the saying goes, “No two days are the same.” In mission-critical environments such as health care, the demands placed on facilities managers require an even rarer level of skill and dedication. Once a health care site becomes operational, the facilities manager assumes full responsibility for its performance. There are no excuses. Regardless of unfinished punch list items, missing record drawings or incomplete equipment information, the facility must function as expected because the lives of patients depend on it.

Adding to this challenge is the diverse range of stakeholders health care facilities managers must satisfy. These stakeholders include end-users, staff and, most critically, patients — each with unique expectations and concerns about the facility’s performance.

Health care facilities are high-stakes, dynamic environments, underscoring the need for continuous improvement in the areas of people, processes and technology. To meet these challenges, health care facilities managers must consistently seek advancements that enhance patient care, optimize end-user satisfaction and improve overall building performance.

## What is a digital twin?

Digital twins have created a tidal wave of buzz in recent years. Although they have been successfully used in various industries, their application for facilities managers — especially health care facilities managers — is distinct and requires a tailored approach. For the built environment, the definition of a digital twin is:

A digital representation of a physical environment, enabling data-driven insights and action.

It represents a connection between the digital and physical world, and it is used to enable organizations to monitor, analyze and simulate real-world data in a virtual environment.

At its core, a digital twin aggregates data from multiple sources to provide actionable insights and support informed decision-making. The true power of a digital twin lies in its ability to create a centralized access point where stakeholders can access, interpret and act on data. This connectivity transforms raw data into meaningful insights, driving measurable improvements in operations, performance and outcomes.

For health care facilities managers, this means unlocking the potential of their facilities’ data to optimize performance, improve stakeholder satisfaction and ultimately enhance patient care.

## Square peg, round hole

The architecture, engineering and construction industry faces an ongoing challenge: the assumption that technologies used during the design and construction phases will seamlessly carry over to the operations phase of a building’s life cycle.

A prominent example is building information modeling (BIM), which has become a powerful tool and the professional standard for design and construction. Over the past decade, BIM has been widely recognized for its transformative potential in building operations. However, its full capabilities and benefits have yet to be fully realized in practice, leaving opportunities for further growth and refinement.

This mismatch often results in a "square peg, round hole" issue, where technology designed for one purpose fails to meet the fundamentally different needs of building operations.

The root of this issue isn’t technology; It’s a human problem. The opportunity lies in truly listening to and understanding the specific needs and challenges of building operations, rather than dictating what technologies should be used. When the tools that solved problems during design and construction are applied without adaptation to operations, they often fall short of addressing the facilities manager’s or chief financial officer’s unique concerns. This mismatch can breed distrust in technology, as it appears incapable of solving operational challenges.

However, this misalignment shouldn’t discourage the use of technology. Instead, it should be seen as an opportunity to engage with building operations teams, define their specific problems and identify which technologies or components of those technologies can genuinely optimize workflows and address pain points.

Revisiting the "square peg" analogy, perhaps the solution isn’t to force the entire square peg into the round hole but to refine it, delivering only the parts that are relevant and adaptable. By tailoring technology to fit operational needs, the field can bridge the gap between design, construction and operations, fostering trust and delivering meaningful value.

## Value proposition through stakeholders

While health care facilities managers face unique challenges in their day-to-day operations, they are ultimately responsible for the performance of their facilities and the impact of those facilities on daily operations. This responsibility underscores the fact that digital transformation and the application of digital twins in building operations cannot stop at addressing the immediate problems faced by the facilities team. Instead, it must extend to understanding how these tools and processes can also help stakeholders, end-users and patients solve their challenges.

The value of a digital twin increases significantly when stakeholders are actively identified and engaged to define use cases. By involving these diverse groups in strategy sessions, facilities managers can uncover insights that lead to alignment between various stakeholders. This collaborative approach ensures that digital twins are tailored to meet the needs of all client types, driving meaningful improvements in building performance and the overall experience for everyone involved.

Commonly recognized stakeholder groups, such as capital projects, maintenance and asset management departments, are often the focus when considering digital twins. However, the applications and value of digital twins extend far beyond these areas. The definition of "stakeholders" can vary by context, but in health care facilities management, stakeholders typically include departments or groups responsible for supporting building operations. By engaging with primary stakeholders within the facilities team, organizations can identify key contributors and progressively expand the stakeholder network as new use cases emerge.

This iterative approach ensures that digital twin initiatives remain aligned with the needs of the facility while fostering greater collaboration and buy-in across the organization. Over time, this process not only strengthens the adoption of digital twin technology but also broadens its impact across diverse operational and strategic objectives.

A list of health care organization stakeholders that could benefit from digital twins are detailed in the graphic below:

![slideshow title](https://www.hfmmagazine.com/sites/default/files/2025-02/StakeholdersDiagram_1050.png)

**Click image to expand**

## Building a case

Defining use cases for stakeholders begins with one essential action: listening. Through ongoing conversations and stakeholder engagement, areas where digital twins can bring value and optimization to facilities departments are revealed. In health care, typical use cases often align with key stakeholder needs, such as:

**Asset identification, location and performance analysis**

Digital twins enable virtual identification of asset locations, providing technicians with critical information before performing maintenance. Beyond this, digital twins can analyze cross-referenced data from the facility’s building management system (BMS) and computerized maintenance management system (CMMS).

For example, if an asset frequently requires maintenance after its temperature exceeds 85 degrees, the digital twin can detect this trend by correlating maintenance data from the CMMS with temperature readings from the BMS. This insight allows proactive measures to prevent future failures and improve the asset management department’s processes and workflows.

**Sustainability and energy**

Hospitals are increasingly required to meet stringent energy-consumption requirements as part of evolving energy regulations. A digital twin can integrate with these benchmarks, comparing real-time facility energy usage to regulatory targets. This integration provides actionable insights, enabling stakeholders or facilities managers to make timely, informed decisions to reduce energy consumption and ensure compliance.

**Environmental services and infection control**

Patient room sanitation is vital for infection control, but current processes often rely on manual checklists verified by managers, leaving room for errors such as missed rooms or incomplete cleaning. Digital twins can address these challenges by providing a visual representation of spaces (both in 2D and 3D) with color-coded status updates for sanitization.

Once cleaning is validated, the system can automatically integrate with the compliance software, filing the required report seamlessly. This improves accuracy, reduces manual effort and ensures adherence to infection control standards.

**Compliance**

Preparing for a compliance survey by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) or an accrediting organization often requires an extensive effort, typically including the creation of a dedicated "war room" for facilities documentation. Digital twins streamline this process by acting as a centralized compliance module that aggregates data from the BMS, CMMS, compliance systems and record documents. For example, facilities managers can quickly access past audit reports and corrective actions taken, providing clear, organized data to address audit concerns efficiently.

These examples represent a small portion of potential use cases for health care facilities. At the core of each is the ability to listen to stakeholders and identify areas where their daily workflows can be improved.

![slideshow title](https://www.hfmmagazine.com/sites/default/files/2025-02/StakeholderUseCases_1050.png)

**Click image to expand**

## A shared benefit

Identifying use cases for a specific stakeholder is a low-hanging fruit that many facilities can immediately reap when first implementing a digital twin. However, stakeholder engagements often reveal use cases that benefit multiple stakeholders or even all stakeholders. When a use case delivers value beyond a single group, its impact multiplies exponentially.

Varying departments in a health care organization often rely on separate access points for data collection and extraction, which increases the risk of outdated or inaccurate information. One common example is the management of record documents for a facility. Recordkeeping documents are often scattered across various locations and systems, quickly becoming outdated after the building becomes operational. Despite this, health care facilities stakeholders — including facilities managers, technicians and compliance teams — cannot afford to let missing or inaccurate information hinder their ability to keep the facility running effectively.

As a result, stakeholders often develop their own processes, document repositories and tracking systems to compensate for the lack of a unified solution. While these workarounds are necessary, they introduce inefficiencies, redundancies and an increased risk of errors.

Digital twins, however, act as a centralized access point, integrating data from various sources into one cohesive system. By aligning multiple stakeholders through a unified digital twin process, facilities can significantly reduce such risks, enabling more effective collaboration, improved decision-making and optimized facility performance.

When a use case supports multiple stakeholders, it amplifies the return on investment in digital twin technology. The more broadly the use case addresses shared challenges, the greater its value. Centralizing access to facilities information is just one example of how digital twins can transform health care facilities management by aligning and empowering all stakeholders.

## Embracing the shift

Digital twins represent a paradigm shift in how health care facilities manage operations, empowering stakeholders with centralized, accurate and actionable data. By addressing shared challenges like fragmented information systems and outdated records, digital twins enable improved collaboration, increased efficiency and enhanced decision-making across departments.

As health care facilities managers begin their journey with digital twins, focusing on stakeholder engagement and identifying shared use cases will maximize the technology's value. By aligning diverse needs through a unified platform, health care facilities can overcome operational silos, optimize performance, and ultimately improve outcomes for patients, staff and organizations alike.

***Jacob D’Albora,** FMP, is a subject matter expert in building data and its application during building operations, with a focus on health care facilities management and driving innovation in the architecture, engineering and construction industry.  He is the director of digital building operations at VIATechnik, a consulting and services firm dedicated to advancing the digital transformation in the built environment.  He can be reached at*[*Jacob.dalbora@viatechnik.com*](mailto:Jacob.dalbora@viatechnik.com "mailto:Jacob.dalbora@viatechnik.com")*.*

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://www.gao.gov/assets/820/817486.png
DESCRIPTION: The diagram illustrates the concept of Digital Twin technology, contrasting the physical twin and digital twin while highlighting data collection, management, and analytics processes essential for informed decision-making.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://brownandcaldwell.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/conventional-vs-digital-twin-brown-and-caldwell.png
DESCRIPTION: The image compares traditional resource management approaches based on static datasets with advanced digital twin systems that incorporate real-time, meta data, enable continuous model updating, and support automated interpretation and actions for social systems.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://social-innovation.hitachi/-/media/project/hitachi/sib/ja-jp/article/digital-twin/image/index-img-02-en.jpg?la=en&upd=20230328070009Z&hash=C1AB41A1B2D4ADEC9DDF2F7656A7DFFB
DESCRIPTION: A visual representation illustrates the concept of digital twin technology, showing the flow of data between a real world, depicted with various symbols like people, vehicles, and buildings, and a virtual world that mirrors these elements while emphasizing the interconnection and feedback between the two environments.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://a.storyblok.com/f/122804/3334x2078/3721cf337e/digital-twin-system.webp
DESCRIPTION: A digital twin system is illustrated, comprising hardware components like sensors and actuators, alongside a middleware layer for data storage, processing, and modeling, and software features focused on designing and analytics.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://www.iwmi.cgiar.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Digital-Twin.png
DESCRIPTION: The illustration depicts the transition from a physical world, including farms, houses, and agricultural activities, to a digital twin that utilizes sensors and communication devices to manage and analyze social and resource systems.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Digital twins enable real-time monitoring and predictive analytics, allowing cities to optimize resource allocation, reduce waste, and improve operational efficiency in urban systems.
By simulating urban environments, digital twins enhance public service accessibility, ensuring equitable access to transportation, utilities, and amenities, fostering inclusivity in city planning.

Digital twins facilitate transparent communication between city officials and residents, encouraging community involvement in decision-making and fostering trust through accessible data visualization.
